She feels like she's 'working' for 5 hours but only produces one paragraph. Her friction point is 'Digital FOMO'—the physical anxiety she feels when she isn't reachable via group chat.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Heavy workload of 100+ pages of reading per week; noisy library environment; feels pressured to respond to group projects instantly.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Phone must be in a different room or a locked bag; all social media tabs must be closed.

tasks.description: Send a quick message to her project groups: 'Focusing for 30 mins, back soon.' This eliminates the guilt and anxiety of being 'offline'.
Open only the word processor. If she needs to look up a quote, she must write [CITATE LATER] and keep writing. This avoids the 'Wikipedia Rabbit Hole'.
If she gets stuck, she must move to a physical notepad for 2 minutes to sketch out the idea before returning to the keyboard.
Record the word count produced in just that 15-minute block. Realizing she did more in 15 mins of 'Deep Work' than 2 hours of 'Multi-tasking' will break the habit.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Reflective Journaling' to improve social intelligence
description: Marcus is a 22-year-old engineering student who feels like he 'puts his foot in his mouth' during team projects. He is technically gifted but often misses social cues, leading to awkward silences or unintended offense. His friction point is 'Defensiveness'—when a social interaction goes poorly, he blames the other person rather than reflecting on his own communication style.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a high-pressure studio environment; works late-night shifts at the library; limited privacy.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Journal must be digital (password protected) for total privacy; focus on one interaction per day.

tasks.description: Write down exactly what was said during one brief interaction today. Avoid using emotional language; just record the dialogue as if it were a script.
Think of one alternative way he could have phrased his part of the conversation. How would that have changed the other person's body language?
Write one sentence describing what the other person might have been feeling (e.g., 'They might have been stressed about their own deadline'). This breaks his habit of blaming.
Write down one 'Social Goal' for tomorrow (e.g., 'I will ask one clarifying question before giving my opinion').

goal: Spend 20 minutes daily learning 'Cloud Fundamentals' via free online labs
description: Sasha is a 19-year-old IT student who feels the curriculum is 'too theoretical.' She wants to have hands-on experience with AWS or Azure but is terrified of accidentally running up a huge bill on her credit card. Her friction point is 'Technical Intimidation'—she feels like if she doesn't understand the whole cloud architecture immediately, she shouldn't even start.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited laptop processing power; shared Wi-Fi that can be spotty in the evenings; no budget for paid courses.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use 'Free Tier' accounts only; must set a billing alarm at $0.01 as the very first task.

tasks.description: Log into the cloud console and navigate to the 'Billing' dashboard. Set an email alert for any spending over $0.01. This eliminates her primary friction point (fear of cost).
Pick exactly one service (e.g., S3 or EC2). Read the first page of the 'Quick Start' guide and locate that service in the console. Do not launch anything yet.
Practice launching a 'Micro' instance and then immediately terminating it. Knowing exactly how to 'Turn it Off' gives her the confidence to explore further tomorrow.
Write one cloud term learned today (e.g., 'Bucket', 'Instance', 'Region') on a sticky note and put it on her monitor. This builds her technical vocabulary over time.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying for 'Off-Campus' fellowships or entry-level roles
description: Raj is a 21-year-old international student on an F-1 visa. He is terrified of the '90-day unemployment rule' post-graduation. He spends hours on Reddit reading horror stories about visa rejections instead of actually applying for jobs. His friction point is 'Bureaucratic Paralysis'—the sheer volume of paperwork and the fear of ticking the wrong box on a sponsorship question makes him close his laptop in a panic.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must maintain a 3.5 GPA to keep his scholarship; works 20 hours/week in the campus IT department; limited budget for professional headshots.
baseline.physical_constraints: High cortisol-induced acid reflux; needs to avoid late-night caffeine.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must verify every company's E-Verify status before applying; no 'ghost-writing' applications—must be his own voice.

tasks.description: Spend the first 5 minutes searching the E-Verify database for three companies he found on LinkedIn. If they aren't on it, move on immediately. This prevents wasting time on non-sponsoring firms.

Create a 'Standard Responses' document for common visa-related questions (e.g., 'Will you now or in the future require sponsorship?'). Having the 'correct' text ready reduces the anxiety of the 'Sponsorship' checkbox.
Log into one company portal and fill out only the 'Personal Information' and 'Education' sections. Save the draft. This lowers the barrier to completion by splitting the task over two days.
After closing the job portal, Raj is allowed 10 minutes of gaming but is strictly forbidden from visiting immigration forums. This protects his mental health from the 'doom-scrolling' cycle.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Progressive Exposure' to classroom participation
description: Sloane is a 19-year-old freshman with 'selective mutism' tendencies in academic settings. She knows the answers but her throat feels 'tight' whenever she thinks about raising her hand. Her grades are suffering because 20% of the syllabus is 'participation'. Her friction point is 'The Spotlight Effect'—she is convinced that if she stumbles on a word, the entire lecture hall of 200 people will laugh and remember it forever.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Three seminars per week with required discussion; seats are assigned in the front row of one class.
baseline.physical_constraints: Severe social anxiety-induced dry mouth; shaky hands.
baseline.non_negotiables: No skipping class to avoid speaking; must keep a bottle of water on the desk at all times.

tasks.description: During the lecture, make eye contact with the professor and nod twice when they make a point. This is 'silent participation' that builds a non-verbal bridge with the instructor.
Approach the professor after the lecture ends and ask one simple, pre-written question (e.g., 'Will this slide be posted online?'). This practices speaking in the room without the audience.
If the class has a digital component or a Zoom link, type one question or 'Thank you' in the chat. This allows her to contribute 'textually' before moving to 'vocal' participation.
In the bathroom before class, hum a low note for 30 seconds to relax the vocal cords and the 'tight' feeling in her throat.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily learning 'Critical Academic Research' and Fact-Checking
description: Leo is a 20-year-old Communications major who has been accused of using 'unreliable sources' in his last two papers. He relies heavily on the first three results of a Google search and often quotes 'opinion pieces' as facts. His friction point is 'Cognitive Laziness'—he finds the university library database slow and 'annoying' to navigate, so he defaults to easier, less credible paths.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited high-speed data; shared laptop with a sibling; library is across campus.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use 'Google Scholar' or the University library search for all assignments; no quoting blogs or Wikipedia directly.

tasks.description: Practice using one advanced search operator (e.g., 'site:.edu' or 'AROUND(5)') to find a specific paper. This turns the 'annoying' search into a game of efficiency.
For one source found, look up the author's credentials on LinkedIn or the university staff page. Knowing 'who' wrote it helps him understand 'why' they wrote it.
Find a good paper, skip to the 'References' section, and pick one source the *author* cited. This 'source-chaining' is faster than starting a new search from scratch.
Identify one 'emotional' word in an article. If the article uses 'disastrous' or 'wonderful' instead of data, he must label it as 'biased' in his notes.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Dorm-Room' bodyweight strength training
description: Zoe is a 20-year-old student who wants to feel stronger but has 'Gym Phobia.' She feels like everyone is watching her and judging her form. She bought a pair of 5lb dumbbells but they are currently being used as bookends. Her friction point is 'Self-Consciousness'—she is worried her roommates will think she's 'being extra' if she starts working out in their shared space.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Triple-occupancy dorm room; no elevator; no privacy except for the shared bathroom stalls.
baseline.physical_constraints: Weak core stability; prone to 'locking' her knees.
baseline.non_negotiables: No equipment that makes noise (e.g., no jumping); must be done in pajamas or lounge clothes to lower the 'barrier to entry'.

tasks.description: Perform a 30-second plank while her roommates are focused on their own desks or phones. It’s a quiet, low-profile move that doesn't draw attention.
Use the edge of her dorm bed to perform 10 slow dips. This utilizes existing furniture and avoids the need for a 'gym' setup.
Perform a 45-second wall sit while reading a single page of her textbook. Linking the 'hard' physical task to her 'productive' study task makes it feel more acceptable.
Perform 10 slow 'cat-cow' movements on her bed to decompress the spine after a day of hunching over a laptop.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Active Recovery' for wrist and neck strain
description: Kenji is a 22-year-old gamer and CS student who has started experiencing 'numbness' in his pinky and ring finger (Ulnar Nerve compression). He is terrified he is developing permanent damage but doesn't want to stop gaming. His friction point is 'Denial'—he ignores the pain until it becomes a 'lightning bolt' sensation, then stops everything for a day, then goes right back to the bad habit.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Spends 10+ hours a day at a desk; non-ergonomic setup; limited budget for a better chair or mouse.
baseline.physical_constraints: Cubital tunnel syndrome symptoms; tight scalene muscles in the neck.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must perform 'Nerve Glides' every morning and evening; no 'power-gaming' sessions longer than 90 minutes without a break.

tasks.description: Perform the 'OK' sign flip (holding hand to ear like a mask) 10 times slowly. This gently pulls the nerve through the elbow tunnel without over-stretching it.
Perform 15 chin tucks (creating a 'double chin') while sitting at the PC. This takes the pressure off the nerves in the neck that lead down to the arm.
Use the opposite thumb to find 'tender spots' in the forearm and apply light pressure for 30 seconds. This releases the muscles pulling on the wrist tendons.
Submerge the elbow in cold water for 2 minutes after a long coding session. This reduces the inflammation he usually ignores until it's too late.

goal: Establish a consistent 3-step 'Circadian Reset' morning routine
description: Maya is a 20-year-old film student who suffers from 'Social Jetlag.' She stays up editing until 2 AM and then sleeps through her 10 AM classes with the curtains blacked out. She feels like a 'zombie' until mid-afternoon. Her friction point is the 'cozy trap'—her bed is the only comfortable place in her cramped dorm, so she often checks her phone for an hour under the covers instead of getting up.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dorm room has a single window facing a brick wall (limited direct sun); shared bathroom is down a long hallway.
baseline.physical_constraints: Extreme light sensitivity in the morning; prone to 'migraine hangovers' if she wakes up dehydrated.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be out of the dorm building by 9:30 AM; no caffeine until after the first 16oz of water.

tasks.description: Immediately upon the alarm, stand up and pin the curtains open. If it's dark, turn on a 10,000-lux SAD lamp for 5 minutes while sitting on the edge of the bed.
Drink 16oz of water with a pinch of sea salt. This replenishes electrolytes lost during her late-night editing sessions and reduces morning brain fog.
Perform 10 walking lunges in the dorm hallway on the way to the communal bathroom. The physical movement signals to the body that the 'sleep' phase is officially over.
Do not open any social media or email apps until she has physically left the dorm building. This prevents the 'scroll-hole' that leads to skipping class.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily on 'Morning Mindset' journaling to reduce academic anxiety
description: Liam is a 21-year-old Pre-Med senior. He wakes up with an immediate 'racing heart' and a mental loop of all the ways he might fail his upcoming exams. He feels like he is already 'behind' the second he opens his eyes. His friction point is 'Toxic Productivity'—he feels that if he isn't studying, he's failing, which makes a 10-minute journal session feel like an unbearable waste of time.

baseline.fixed_constraints: MCAT prep courses on Saturdays; library 'deep-work' shifts; lives in a loud fraternity house.
baseline.physical_constraints: Elevated morning cortisol; shallow breathing patterns.
baseline.non_negotiables: Journaling must be done in a physical notebook to avoid the 'distraction' of a laptop.

tasks.description: Write down the three biggest worries for the day. Explicitly label them: 'This is an anxiety, not a fact.' This externalization reduces the physical panic response.
Write one sentence about *why* you are pursuing medicine that isn't about grades (e.g., 'I want to help families like mine'). This reconnects him to his purpose.
Identify the absolute smallest first step for his hardest task (e.g., 'Open the Bio-Chem book to page 40'). Focus only on that step to stop the overwhelm.
Perform 4 rounds of Box Breathing while holding the closed journal. This physically lowers his heart rate before he opens his textbook.

goal: Perform a 15-minute 'Active Readiness' bodyweight circuit before the first lecture
description: Sam is a 19-year-old student who has become 'sedentary' since starting college. He sits for 10+ hours a day and has started developing 'tech-neck' and tight hips. He wants to exercise but finds the campus gym too crowded and intimidating in the mornings. His friction point is 'Self-Consciousness'—he is worried his roommates will judge him for doing squats in the kitchen area.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Small shared living room; morning lecture at 9 AM; limited budget for workout gear.
baseline.physical_constraints: Anterior pelvic tilt (lower back arching); weak core.
baseline.non_negotiables: No jumping or loud movements (to respect neighbors/roommates); no equipment except a chair.

tasks.description: Perform a 45-second wall sit while waiting for the microwave or the communal shower. This builds leg strength silently and discreetly.
Lie on the floor and place heels on his desk chair. Perform 20 slow glute bridges. This reverses the hip-tightness caused by hours of sitting.
Use the bathroom door frame to perform a 60-second pectoral stretch. This counteracts the 'hunched' posture from laptop usage.
Stand against the wall and ensure heels, glutes, and head touch the surface. Carry this 'tall' feeling to the first class of the day.

goal: Prepare a 'High-Focus' lunch and 64oz water bottle every morning
description: Chloe is a 22-year-old Senior who 'forgets to eat' during her 6-hour lab blocks and then binges on vending machine snacks. She experiences extreme afternoon 'brain fog' and irritability. Her friction point is 'Decision Fatigue'—by the time she wakes up, she is so stressed about her lab reports that she can't decide what to pack, so she leaves with nothing.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited fridge space; shared kitchen is messy in the mornings; strictly 20 minutes for the actual morning prep.
baseline.physical_constraints: Prone to low-blood-sugar dizziness.
baseline.non_negotiables: Lunch must be 'cold-stable' (no microwave needed in the lab); must include at least 20g of protein.

tasks.description: Fill a 64oz jug with water and ice the moment she enters the kitchen. Adding lemon or mint makes it 'rewarding' enough to actually drink.
Place a pre-boiled egg or a packet of tuna in the lunchbox before anything else. This ensures the 'Non-Negotiable' protein is present.
Toss a handful of baby carrots or pre-washed spinach into a container. No chopping allowed—keep the friction for vegetables at zero.
Mark four 16oz 'milestone' lines on the bottle. She must reach the first line by 10 AM. This gamifies her hydration during labs.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily on 'Zero-Downtime' skill practice (Coding or Language)
description: Zack is a 19-year-old student trying to learn Python outside of his non-STEM major. He feels like he 'never has a big block of time' to study, so he never starts. His friction point is 'The Perfect Setup'—he thinks he needs his dual monitors, special keyboard, and coffee to code, which means he only practices once every two weeks.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Spends 40 minutes a day on the campus shuttle; relies on a tablet during the day.
baseline.physical_constraints: Prone to motion sickness if reading small text on the bus.
baseline.non_negotiables: Practice must be possible on a mobile device or tablet; must focus on logic rather than syntax 'perfection'.

tasks.description: While on the bus, use a mobile coding app (like Mimo or Sololearn) to solve two logic puzzles. If motion sickness occurs, switch to a technical podcast.
On a physical index card, draw a flow-chart for a simple program (e.g., 'If temperature > 70, print Wear Shorts'). This trains the brain to think in code without a screen.
Even if it's just one line of code or one flashcard, he must complete it before his 11 AM class. This protects the 'identity' of being a learner.
Explain the concept of 'Variables' or 'Loops' out loud to himself while walking to the dining hall. If he can explain it, he knows it.

goal: Establish a consistent 30-minute 'Phone-Free' morning study block
description: Amara is a 19-year-old engineering student who wakes up and immediately checks her notifications, leading to 45 minutes of 'bed-scrolling.' By the time she actually gets up, she is already behind schedule and feels mentally cluttered. Her friction point is 'Digital Dependency'—she uses her phone as her primary alarm, which places the source of distraction in her hand the moment she wakes up. She feels anxious if she isn't 'up to date' on her group chats before class.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Communal bathroom is often crowded at 8 AM; 8:30 AM lecture start time; lives in a high-noise dorm.
baseline.physical_constraints: Severe morning grogginess (sleep inertia).
baseline.non_negotiables: No social media usage until 8:15 AM; phone must be placed in a 'charging station' across the room.

tasks.description: Purchase and use a cheap, analog alarm clock. Place the smartphone in a desk drawer at 11 PM to decouple the 'wake up' signal from the 'distraction' device.
Drink 16oz of water kept on the desk immediately after the alarm. This helps combat the sleep inertia that usually drives her back into her phone for 'stimulation'.
Open a physical textbook or one pre-downloaded PDF. Review the summary of the previous lecture for 15 minutes while the room is quiet and the phone is still in the drawer.
After the 15-minute review is complete, she is allowed 5 minutes of phone usage while walking to the communal bathroom as a reward for the deep work block.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily on 'Morning Mobility' to counteract heavy backpack strain
description: Sanjay is a 20-year-old student who commutes by bus and carries a 25lb laptop bag for 8 hours a day. He wakes up with a 'stiff' neck and a recurring ache in his right shoulder. He thinks stretching is 'for athletes' and doesn't consider himself one. His friction point is 'Self-Consciousness'—he feels weird doing stretches in his shared bedroom while his roommates are sleeping.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited floor space in the room; must leave for the bus at 7:45 AM; shared bathroom has a long wait.
baseline.physical_constraints: Forward head posture; limited shoulder rotation; tight trapezius muscles.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be standing-based exercises (no floor contact); must be silent (no jumping).

tasks.description: Perform 10 slow 'chin tucks' while brushing teeth. This uses a non-negotiable habit to strengthen the deep neck flexors and fix 'tech neck'.
On the way out of the room, place both forearms on the doorframe and lean forward for 30 seconds. This opens the chest and counters the 'backpack hunch'.
While waiting for the bus, pull shoulder blades back and down 15 times as if trying to put them in your back pockets. This activates the mid-back for better posture.
Consciously switch the bag to the left shoulder for the first 5 minutes of the walk to class. This prevents chronic asymmetry in his shoulder height.

goal: Transition to a 'Dorm-Friendly' low-sugar morning hydration routine
description: Liam is a freshman who has gained 10 lbs because he drinks a 20oz mocha latte every morning from the campus cafe ($7/day). He wants to save money and reduce his sugar spikes which lead to a 2 PM crash. His friction point is 'Sensory Deprivation'—he hates the taste of plain dorm tap water and finds the 'ritual' of the cafe visits to be his only social highlight of the morning.

baseline.fixed_constraints: No stove or kettle in the dorm; limited fridge space; tight student budget.
baseline.physical_constraints: Caffeine withdrawal headaches if he stops coffee entirely.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be under $1 per day; must provide a 'flavor' incentive; must be prepared in under 3 minutes.

tasks.description: Fill a 24oz bottle with water and a bag of fruit tea (no sugar) before bed. By morning, the water is naturally flavored and ready to grab.
Take a different route to his first class that does not pass the campus cafe. This removes the 'visual trigger' that prompts him to spend $7 on sugar.
If the headache starts, he can have one cup of black coffee or tea from the communal pot. No milk or syrup. This maintains the caffeine baseline without the 50g of sugar.
Every Friday, check the bank app and move $30 (the saved cafe money) into a 'Spring Break' savings sub-account. The immediate financial reward reinforces the habit.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Pre-Lecture' focused meditation
description: Chloe is a 21-year-old student who feels her 'brain is a swarm of bees' during morning lectures. She worries about her debt, her parents, and her social standing, often realizing 40 minutes into class that she hasn't heard a word the professor said. Her friction point is 'Self-Critique'—she thinks she is 'bad' at meditating because her mind wanders, leading her to quit after 2 minutes.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes via a loud subway; lecture halls are crowded and distracting; high academic stakes.
baseline.physical_constraints: Elevated heart rate (anxiety); shallow 'chest breathing'.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be done in a public or semi-public space; must use a physical anchor (like a stone or a coin).

tasks.description: While on the commute, hold a small coin or stone in the pocket. Focus entirely on the texture and temperature for 3 minutes to ground her in the present moment.
After finding a seat in class, perform 5 rounds of Box Breathing (4s in, 4s hold, 4s out, 4s hold). This shifts the body from 'survival' mode to 'learning' mode.

When a distracting thought arises (e.g., 'I forgot to pay that bill'), she must mentally say 'Thinking' and return focus to the coin in her hand. This addresses her 'Self-Critique' by making distractions part of the practice.
Write the date and 'Focus Mode: ON' at the top of her notebook. This physical act serves as a 'closing gate' for her personal worries.

goal: Establish a 'Campus-Ready' 20-minute grooming and maintenance routine
description: Jordan is a freshman who has fallen into 'survival mode,' often wearing the same hoodie for four days and skipping basic hygiene because he is 'too busy.' He noticed he feels less confident and more isolated when he looks 'messy.' His friction point is 'Executive Overwhelm'—he views showering, laundry, and skin care as one giant, impossible block of time, rather than individual small tasks.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shared dorm bathroom with limited hot water; high-stress environment; limited personal grooming products.
baseline.physical_constraints: Dry skin and occasional breakouts due to lack of washing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must include daily oral hygiene; must change socks and underwear every 24 hours; no skipping sessions for 'extra' sleep.

tasks.description: Commit to a 5-minute shower immediately after waking. Focus only on 'cleaning,' not 'contemplating life.' Use cold water for the last 30 seconds to force him out of the stall.
Apply a basic moisturizer with SPF immediately after the shower. This protects his skin from cold campus winds and treats the dryness he currently ignores.
While the moisturizer dries, pick one clean t-shirt and fresh socks. Place the 'yesterday' clothes in the hamper immediately to prevent the 'floor-pile' from growing.
Spend 10 seconds looking in the mirror after grooming and acknowledging the effort. This builds the 'Self-Care Identity' he is currently lacking.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying 'Spaced Repetition' to flashcards for midterms
description: Ben is a 19-year-old pre-law student who is drowning in Latin terminology and case law. He has a habit of 'cramming'—studying for 6 hours straight the night before a quiz—which leads to high stress and zero long-term retention. His friction point is 'Mental Friction'; he finds the initial struggle of remembering a card frustrating and often cheats by looking at the answer too early. He also feels that 15 minutes is 'not enough' to be productive, leading him to skip the habit entirely.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 30 minutes by bus; limited desk space in a quad-dorm; constant noise from roommates.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but prone to digital eye strain from excessive laptop use.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a dedicated Spaced Repetition System (SRS) like Anki or Quizlet; cards must be reviewed before 11 AM to ensure the brain is fresh.

tasks.description: While waiting for the campus shuttle, open the app and download the day's 'due' cards. This ensures the material is ready the moment he sits down.
When a card appears, he must close his eyes and try to recall the answer for exactly 5 seconds before flipping. This 'desirable difficulty' is what builds the memory.
Any card that takes more than two attempts to remember must be 'flagged.' He will spend the last 2 minutes of the session specifically re-reading the textbook context for just those flagged cards.
Take a screenshot of the 'No more cards due' screen and send it to a study buddy. This external accountability makes the 15-minute habit feel like a finished 'mission'.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Morning Mental Unloading' via a brain-dump journal
description: Lydia is a 21-year-old senior who wakes up with a 'racing mind.' She immediately starts worrying about her thesis, her graduation dress, and her student loan balance. This 'morning static' makes her feel paralyzed, often leading her to stay in bed for an hour. Her friction point is 'Self-Censorship'—she tries to write 'pretty' or 'meaningful' journal entries, which makes the process feel like another graded assignment.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Morning yoga class at 7 AM; shares a room with a light sleeper; limited privacy.
baseline.physical_constraints: Elevated morning heart rate (anxiety-linked); shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a physical notebook; handwriting can be illegible; no one else is allowed to read it.

tasks.description: Set a timer for 60 seconds and write down every single 'to-do' or 'worry' currently in the head. No sentences required—just a list of fragments.
Write one brutally honest sentence about how she feels right now (e.g., 'I am terrified of failing'). Naming the emotion reduces its power over her day.
Identify three actionable items from the 'Messy Minute' and move them to a sticky note. The rest of the list is mentally 'discarded' for the morning.
Physically close the notebook and place it in a drawer. This physical action signals to the brain that the 'worrying phase' is over and the 'action phase' has begun.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Professional Body Language' for senior interviews
description: Kevin is a 22-year-old engineering senior who is brilliant on paper but 'collapses' in person. He mumbles, hunches his shoulders, and fidgets with his pen, making him appear nervous and unreliable to recruiters. He wants to project 'Executive Presence' but feels like a 'fraud' when he tries to sit up straight. His friction point is 'Physical Discomfort'—his back muscles are weak from years of gaming, making good posture literally tiring.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Interviews are usually on Zoom; limited professional attire; shared bedroom with no full-length mirror.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (forward head posture); tight hamstrings; weak core.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the laptop camera for self-review; sessions must be standing or seated-active (no lying down).

tasks.description: Stand against a wall with heels, glutes, and head touching. Perform 10 'Wall Angels' (sliding arms up and down) to open the chest and realign the spine.
Open the webcam. Practice sitting in the 'Power Box' (hands visible but not frantic, head in the upper third of the frame). Speak one paragraph of his 'About Me' intro.
While speaking on camera, keep hands resting flat on the desk or loosely clasped. If he feels the urge to fidget, he must squeeze his toes instead (invisible to the camera).
Practice looking directly into the camera lens (not the screen) and giving a brief, 2-second smile. This builds 'digital warmth' and trust with the interviewer.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Low-Impact' morning movement to manage back pain
description: Zoe is a 20-year-old architecture student who recently started having lower back 'spasms' from sitting 12 hours a day at a drafting table. She is scared of 'real' exercise because she doesn't want to make the pain worse. She feels 'clunky' and uncoordinated. Her friction point is 'Fear-Avoidance'—whenever her back feels slightly stiff, she stops moving entirely, which actually increases the stiffness.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor dorm with no elevator; limited floor space; uses a heavy backpack.
baseline.physical_constraints: Anterior pelvic tilt; limited hip mobility; tight hip flexors.
baseline.non_negotiables: No jumping; no heavy lifting; must use a yoga mat or a soft rug.

tasks.description: Perform 10 slow Cat-Cow cycles on the floor. Focus on the breath and moving one vertebra at a time to reduce the 'spasm' tension.
Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining a flat back (5 reps per side). This builds the 'internal corset' that supports her spine during long drafting sessions.
Perform a gentle lunge stretch for 30 seconds per side. This 'unlocks' the hips that have been shortened by a day of sitting.
Write down if the movement felt like 'Good Discomfort' (stretching) or 'Bad Pain' (sharp). Recognizing the difference helps her overcome her 'fear-avoidance'.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Financial Tracking' to eliminate overdraft fees
description: Sami is a 19-year-old freshman who has overdrafted his account three times this month. He has a habit of 'ignoring the numbers' and just tapping his phone for every purchase until it gets declined. He feels 'guilty' and 'stupid' about his spending, so he avoids the banking app entirely. His friction point is 'Financial Shame'—he views the low balance as a personal failure rather than a data point.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Income is a $200/month allowance; shared expenses with a roommate; relies on campus meal plan.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must check the bank balance every single morning; no use of 'Buy Now Pay Later' services.

tasks.description: Open the banking app for exactly 30 seconds before getting out of bed. Just look at the number. Do not judge it; just see it. This desensitizes the 'shame' response.
Look through the last 7 days of transactions. Identify one recurring charge (e.g., a streaming service or an app) that he doesn't use daily. Cancel it immediately.
Divide his remaining monthly balance by the number of days left in the month. This gives him a 'Daily Spending Cap.' Write this number on a sticky note and put it on his desk.
On Friday, withdraw $30 in cash. He is only allowed to spend that cash for the weekend. When it's gone, the 'spending window' is closed. This makes the money 'real' again.

goal: Establish a consistent 'Launchpad' morning routine to eliminate school-morning panic
description: Elena is a 21-year-old architecture student with ADHD. She consistently loses her keys, ID card, or sketchbook in her messy dorm, causing her to be 10 minutes late to every 9 AM class. This 'morning panic' triggers her anxiety and makes her skip her first-period lecture entirely out of embarrassment. Her friction point is 'object blindness'—when she is stressed, she literally cannot see the items she is looking for, even if they are in plain sight.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Studio classes run late until 10 PM; shares a tiny dorm with one roommate; minimal storage space.
baseline.physical_constraints: High cortisol in the morning; prone to 'freezing' when overwhelmed.
baseline.non_negotiables: The 'Launchpad' (a dedicated tray/hook) must be near the door; routine must take less than 5 minutes.

tasks.description: At 10:30 PM, empty the backpack of all trash and old coffee cups. Place the laptop, charger, and tomorrow's specific textbooks inside. This prevents the 'where is my book' scramble.
Place keys, student ID, and phone on a dedicated magnetic hook or tray next to the dorm door. Do this immediately upon entering the room at night, no exceptions.
Before touching the doorknob in the morning, touch three items in the 'Launchpad' and say their names out loud: 'Keys, Card, Phone.' Verbalizing confirms the physical presence.
Tape a brightly colored index card to the back of the door: 'If you're late, go anyway.' This targets the emotional friction that causes her to skip class when the routine fails.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily on 'Morning Cognitive Priming' using non-digital logic puzzles
description: Marcus is a 19-year-old math major who feels his brain is 'rotting' from 2 AM doomscrolling. He wakes up and immediately checks TikTok, which leaves him feeling distracted and impulsive during his morning logic classes. He wants to reclaim his focus. His friction point is 'dopamine withdrawal'—without the phone, he feels a sense of profound boredom that makes him feel itchy and irritable.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 20 minutes by bus; needs to be 'alert' for a 9:00 AM proofs class; limited budget for hobby materials.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: No screens until after the first class; must use a physical puzzle book (Sudoku, Crossword, or Logic Grid).

tasks.description: Plug the phone into a charger inside his backpack before bed. It stays zipped in the bag until he reaches his classroom desk.
Open a physical Sudoku book on the bus. Solve one puzzle (Easy level initially to build momentum). The goal is 'linear focus'—one task with a clear beginning and end.
If a puzzle feels too hard, spend 2 minutes just doodling geometric shapes. This keeps the hands engaged and prevents the reflex to reach for the phone in the backpack.
Upon entering the lecture hall, rate 'Brain Fog' from 1 to 10. Comparing 'Phone Mornings' to 'Puzzle Mornings' will reinforce the behavior change.

goal: Engage in 15 minutes of 'Active Social Integration' in the dining hall daily
description: Sita is a 20-year-old transfer student who feels like an outsider. She grabs a bagel and immediately retreats to her room to eat alone while watching Netflix. She wants to make friends but feels like every existing friend group is 'closed.' Her friction point is 'The Spotlight Effect'—she is convinced that if she sits alone at a large table, everyone is looking at her and pitying her.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dining hall is peak busy from 8:15 to 8:45 AM; limited seating during finals; high noise floor.
baseline.physical_constraints: Social-anxiety induced nausea; tends to lose appetite when stressed.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must eat at a communal table (at least 4 seats); no earbuds allowed while in the dining hall.

tasks.description: After getting food, stand by the beverage station for 2 minutes. Scan for a table with at least one empty seat. This allows the heart rate to stabilize before sitting.
Approach a table and use the exact phrase: 'Is anyone sitting here?' This is a low-stakes interaction that doesn't require a full conversation.
Place the phone face-down on the table. It acts as a 'social bridge'—she isn't hiding behind it, but it's there if she needs an 'emergency' exit. Eat for 10 minutes in this state.
When leaving the table, give a brief nod or small smile to the person closest to her. No words needed. This builds 'social presence' over time.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily on 'Morning Administrative Clearance' for internships
description: Jordan is a senior who has a great resume but 'ghosts' recruiters because he is overwhelmed by the back-and-forth emails. He has 150+ unread professional emails. His friction point is 'Task Avoidance'—he views a simple 'Schedule an Interview' email as a two-hour ordeal involving checking his calendar, finding a suit, and researching the company.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use the campus Career Center's Handshake app; limited professional wardrobe; morning 'brain-fog'.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must respond to all 'Direct Messages' within 24 hours; no checking personal social media until 'Admin' is clear.

tasks.description: Search the inbox for keywords: 'Interview', 'Application', 'Scheduling'. Move these to a 'Priority' folder and ignore everything else for this 15-minute block.
Reply to one scheduling request using a pre-written template: 'Hello [Name], thank you for reaching out. I am available at [Time]. Best, Jordan.' Do not overthink the prose.
Immediately mark the interview time on his physical wall calendar and his phone. This prevents the 'I forgot' panic that leads to ghosting.
Archive the handled email. Seeing the number of 'Priority' emails go down provides the visual proof that the task wasn't a two-hour ordeal.

goal: Perform 10 minutes of 'Morning Lower-Body Awakening' to manage long-lecture stiffness
description: Zoe is a 20-year-old student who has 4-hour lab blocks where she is not allowed to sit, followed by 3-hour history seminars where she isn't allowed to stand. She wakes up with 'heavy' legs and throbbing feet. She thinks 'leg day' is only for bodybuilders. Her friction point is 'Pain-Anticipation'—she is so tired in the morning that the idea of moving her legs feels like it will drain her remaining energy.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shared bathroom is far away; heavy backpack; required to wear closed-toe shoes for labs.
baseline.physical_constraints: Occasional swelling in ankles; tight hamstrings; low core stability.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be done while the coffee/tea is steeping; no floor-work (limited space).

tasks.description: While sitting on the edge of the bed, roll each foot over a tennis ball for 2 minutes. This releases the plantar fascia and reduces the 'heavy' feeling in the feet.
While waiting for her water to boil, perform 20 slow heel raises while holding the kitchen counter for balance. This pumps blood back up from the legs.
Gently swing each leg forward and back 10 times while holding a door frame. This dynamic stretch is much easier on the back than a static forward fold.
Put on a pair of light compression socks before heading to the lab. This is a 'passive' habit that supports her 'active' morning work.

goal: Spend 15 minutes every morning reviewing the 'Active Recall' questions from the previous day's lectures
description: Maya is a 20-year-old premed student who suffers from 'The Illusion of Competence.' She spends hours highlighting her textbooks, but when she gets to the exam, she realizes she can't retrieve the information. Her friction point is the 'Mental Strain' of testing herself; it feels much harder and more discouraging than re-reading, so she often defaults to passive review to 'feel' productive.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 30 minutes via noisy shuttle; 8:00 AM chemistry lab twice a week; shared dorm with a 'snooze-button' roommate.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronic tension headaches from screen glare; requires analog (paper) study options.
baseline.non_negotiables: No looking at the answers until the 15-minute timer is up; must be done before the first cup of caffeine.

tasks.description: Place the notebook with self-generated questions on the bedside table before sleeping. This ensures the material is the first thing reached for, rather than the smartphone.
Cover the answer column with a sheet of paper. Attempt to answer three high-level questions from the previous day’s Bio-Chem lecture out loud. This forces the brain to 'work' to find the data.
Uncover the answers and use a red pen to circle exactly where the logic failed. This visual marker prevents the same mistake from recurring during the lab.
Mark a tally on a physical wall calendar for every morning the 'Struggle' was completed. Seeing the streak grow helps override the desire for 'Easy' passive reading.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Social Priming' at the campus coffee shop daily
description: Liam is a 19-year-old freshman who feels socially 'invisible.' He spends his mornings in a hoodie with earbuds in to avoid eye contact, but he desperately wants to make friends. His friction point is 'The Spotlight Effect'—he is convinced that everyone is judging his appearance, causing him to hunch over his phone. He needs a low-stakes way to exist in public without 'hiding.'

baseline.fixed_constraints: Budget limited to one small black coffee ($2.50); campus cafe is peak-busy at 8:45 AM; limited seating.
baseline.physical_constraints: Social-anxiety induced sweating; shaky hands when holding a cup.
baseline.non_negotiables: No earbuds allowed while inside the cafe; phone must stay in the backpack, not the pocket.

tasks.description: Physically put the earbuds inside the backpack before entering the building. This removes the 'social shield' and forces awareness of the surrounding environment.
While waiting for the coffee, identify three people's clothing colors. This 'external' focus reduces the internal rumination about his own appearance.
Make eye contact with the barista and say 'Thank you, have a good one' clearly. This 'micro-interaction' practices his voice and eye contact in a safe, transactional setting.
Sit at a high-top table for 5 minutes. Hands must be visible (on the table) and the back must be straight. No scrolling. Observe the room to see that no one is actually staring at him.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying for 'Work-Study' or on-campus roles
description: Carlos is a first-generation college student who is running out of food money. He is eligible for work-study but is intimidated by the 'Professionalism' required in the applications. He thinks he needs a perfect cover letter for a simple library assistant job. His friction point is 'Administrative Avoidance'—he opens the portal, sees the 'Upload Resume' button, and closes it because his resume feels 'unimpressive.'

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use the 'Handshake' portal; no personal laptop (uses the library computer); work-study eligibility expires soon.
baseline.physical_constraints: Hunger-related fatigue in the mornings.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must apply to at least one job per session; no editing the resume for more than 5 minutes.

tasks.description: Go directly to the campus library computers at 8:00 AM before classes. The 'academic' environment provides the necessary focus he lacks in his noisy dorm.
Pick one job description. Change exactly two words on the resume to match the job's 'Skills' section (e.g., changing 'helped people' to 'customer service').
Hit 'Submit' on the application. Do not re-read the cover letter. The goal is the volume of applications to secure an interview, not a Pulitzer-prize winning essay.
Open his email and confirm the 'Application Received' message. This physical proof of progress acts as a psychological reward.

goal: Execute a 10-minute 'Morning Body-Scan' and neck mobility drill
description: Sarah is a 20-year-old architecture student who wakes up with 'Tech Neck'—a sharp pain at the base of her skull from 10 hours of drafting. She is so used to the pain that she doesn't realize how much it affects her mood until she 'snaps' at her roommates. Her friction point is 'Self-Neglect'—she feels that taking 10 minutes for her body is 'selfish' when she has so much work to do.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Studio shifts often end at 2 AM; shared bathroom; requires high-precision motor skills (drafting) which are impaired by neck pain.
baseline.physical_constraints: Limited neck rotation; tingling in the thumb; tight upper trapezius.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be done standing; no equipment required; no 'power-stretching' (gentle only).

tasks.description: Stand in front of the bathroom mirror. Align the ears over the shoulders. This 'Postural Awareness' is her baseline for the day.
Perform 10 slow 'Chin Tucks' and 5 gentle 'Median Nerve Glides' (palm to wall). This releases the tension that causes her finger tingling.
Squeeze the shoulder blades together 15 times while focusing on a spot on the wall. This activates the mid-back muscles that have been 'turned off' by hunching.
Rate her neck pain from 1-10 on a sticky note. If it's over 5, she must set a 'Standing Break' timer for her studio session later.

goal: Consume 24oz of water and a fiber-rich snack before the first class
description: Zack is a 22-year-old student who 'survives' on energy drinks and vending machine muffins. He has chronic 'brain fog' and digestive issues. He wants to feel 'cleaner' but find 'health food' to be expensive and pretentious. His friction point is 'The Convenience Trap'—it's easier to grab a Red Bull from the campus store than to prepare anything in his tiny dorm kitchen.

baseline.fixed_constraints: No stove; limited fridge space; high-cost campus market; must be edible on the walk to class.
baseline.physical_constraints: Sugar-crash headaches; caffeine dependency.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be under $1.50 per day; must be 'grab-and-go' from his own dorm stash.

tasks.description: Fill a 24oz bottle with water before bed. This eliminates the 'choice' in the morning—the water is already there when the alarm goes off.
Keep a bag of individual almond packs or apples in the backpack side-pocket. No matter how late he is, he must grab one 'Whole Food' before leaving the room.
He must finish the 24oz of water before he crosses the threshold of his first lecture hall. This uses 'Location' as a behavioral anchor.
If he walks past a vending machine, he must touch the water bottle in his bag. This physical touch acts as a 'reminder' that he is already fueled.

goal: Spend 10 minutes every morning on 'Pre-Lecture' active recall of the previous day's core concepts
description: Maya is a 20-year-old Bio-Chem major who suffers from the 'Illusion of Competence'—she highlights her textbooks and feels like she knows the material, but blanks during exams. Her friction point is 'Mental Pain'; active recall is cognitively demanding and makes her feel 'dumb' when she can't remember a fact, leading her to retreat to comfortable, passive re-reading. She also struggles with morning grogginess and defaults to scrolling her phone for an hour before her 9 AM lab.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 20 minutes on a loud campus shuttle; shared dorm with a 'snooze-button' roommate; limited desk space.
baseline.physical_constraints: Severe morning brain fog; requires 20 minutes to 'fully' wake up.
baseline.non_negotiables: No phone usage until the recall session is finished; must use a physical notebook to avoid digital distraction.

tasks.description: Place the study notebook on top of the smartphone before bed. In the morning, she must move the notebook to the desk to reach the phone, creating a physical choice point.
Without looking at notes, write down the 3 most important takeaways from yesterday's hardest class. If stuck, she must 'struggle' for at least 2 minutes before checking the answer.
Open the textbook and use a red pen to fill in the missing details. This visual contrast highlights exactly where her memory failed, targeting future study sessions.
On the shuttle bus, explain one of those 'Red-Pen' concepts out loud to herself (sub-vocalizing). If she can explain it simply, she has mastered the logic.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily on 'Job Market Priming' and professional outreach
description: Marcus is a 21-year-old Senior who is terrified of the 'real world.' He has a great GPA but zero networking. He spends his mornings checking his grades (which are already high) as a form of 'productive procrastination' to avoid LinkedIn. His friction point is 'Imposter Syndrome'—he feels that professionals will see him as a 'bother' or realize he's just a 'clueless student.'

baseline.fixed_constraints: Final semester workload; capstone project takes 10 hours/week; limited professional wardrobe.
baseline.physical_constraints: Social-anxiety induced heart palpitations when cold-emailing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the campus alumni database; no 'easy-apply' button usage—every application must have one custom sentence.

tasks.description: Before checking emails, find one alum on LinkedIn who has a job that looks 'scary' but interesting. Save their profile to a 'Networking' folder.
Send a 2-sentence message to the alum: 'I'm a senior at [University] and admire your work at [Company]. Would you be open to a 10-min Zoom next month?' No pressure to secure a job, just a talk.
Open his master resume and update exactly one bullet point to be more 'result-oriented' (e.g., adding a percentage or a tool used).
Apply to one 'reach' internship. Once the 'Application Received' screen appears, he must close the laptop and do 10 pushups to release the nervous energy.

goal: Establish a 20-minute 'Morning Grounding' routine to reduce campus-social anxiety
description: Chloe is a transfer student who feels like she doesn't belong. She wakes up and immediately checks 'Snap Maps' to see where people were last night, which triggers FOMO and a feeling of isolation. By the time she reaches her 10 AM class, she is 'mentally exhausted' from social comparison. Her friction point is 'Comparison-itis'—she views her life as a 'Behind-the-Scenes' reel while everyone else is a 'Highlight' reel.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dorm has thin walls (constant noise); shared bathroom; morning commute involves walking through a crowded quad.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tension headaches; shallow breathing when in public spaces.
baseline.non_negotiables: No social media apps until after the first class of the day; must be physically outside for at least 5 minutes.

tasks.description: Sit by the dorm window for 5 minutes. Identify 3 things she can hear outside and 3 things she can see. This 'External Focus' interrupts the internal rumination cycle.
Walk to the dining hall without earbuds. Instead of hiding in a podcast, she must listen to the campus sounds. This 'Ambient Exposure' reduces her fear of social noise.
Give a brief nod or small smile to exactly one person (a staff member, a bus driver, or a student). This builds 'Social Presence' without the pressure of a conversation.
Write one sentence in a pocket journal: 'I am a student at [University] and I have a right to be here.' Carrying the physical notebook acts as a 'talisman' during class.

goal: Spend 10 minutes every morning performing 'Posture Reset' and lower-back stabilization
description: Zack is a 19-year-old student who spends 8 hours a day in non-ergonomic library chairs. He wakes up with a 'stiff' lower back and 'hunched' shoulders that make him feel 40 years older than he is. He thinks the gym is 'too loud' and 'too public.' His friction point is 'Self-Consciousness'—he feels silly doing stretches in the shared dorm kitchen, so he skips them and remains in pain.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited floor space in the room; morning lecture at 9:00 AM; requires a heavy backpack for the commute.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tight hamstrings; anterior pelvic tilt (lower back arching); tech-neck.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be done in pajamas to lower the barrier to entry; no jumping or loud movements.

tasks.description: Sit on the edge of the bed and perform 10 slow pelvic tilts. This wakes up the core and 'unlocks' the lower back before he stands up.
Stand against the only available flat wall in the room. Perform 15 'Wall Angels'—sliding arms up and down like a snow angel. This realigns the 'hunched' drafting-shoulders.
While brushing teeth, perform 20 isometric glute squeezes. This counteracts the pelvic tilt and provides 'invisible' training while in the shared bathroom.
Tighten both backpack straps so the bag sits high on the back. This uses the weight of his books to pull his shoulders back rather than down.

goal: Consume 30g of protein and 24oz of water within 60 minutes of waking
description: Jordan is a freshman who lives on energy drinks and 'vending machine muffins.' He experiences an extreme 2 PM 'crash' every day, leading him to nap and miss his evening study group. He wants to feel 'steady' but finds 'healthy eating' expensive and time-consuming. His friction point is 'Morning Friction'—he is so groggy that if a meal takes more than 2 minutes to prepare, he skips it.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shared dorm-fridge is tiny; no stove access; 15-minute walk to the dining hall.
baseline.physical_constraints: Caffeine dependency; high metabolism; prone to 'hangry' irritability.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be portable (can eat while walking); under $2.00 per serving; no added sugars in the first meal.

tasks.description: Fill a shaker bottle with 1 serving of protein powder and 24oz of water before bed. Place it on the desk. This eliminates the 'decision' to hydrate.
Drink the entire 24oz shaker within 5 minutes of the alarm. This 'shocks' the digestive system and provides the first 20-25g of protein immediately.
Grab 2 pre-boiled eggs (from a weekend batch) to eat on the walk to the first class. This adds the final 12g of protein and healthy fats for brain function.
Set a phone alarm for 2:30 PM to rate alertness (1-10). Realizing he isn't crashing will reinforce the habit over the weekend muffins.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily reading and annotating a physical 'Success Skills' book
description: Diego is a 20-year-old student athlete who feels like he is 'just drifting' through his major. He spends his mornings in a state of 'digital reactive mode,' answering texts and watching highlight reels before he even gets out of bed. His friction point is 'Attention Span Decay'—he finds it physically painful to focus on a single page of text for more than two minutes without checking his phone for a dopamine hit.

baseline.fixed_constraints: 6 AM weightlifting sessions; shared locker room environment; limited personal quiet time.
baseline.physical_constraints: High physical fatigue; occasional 'brain fog' from early morning training.
baseline.non_negotiables: No digital version of the book (must be physical); phone must be in another room; must happen before he checks social media.

tasks.description: Place the smartphone in a desk drawer and place the physical book on the pillow. The book is the only allowed 'stimulus' for the first 15 minutes after returning from the gym.
For every page read, he must write one question or one disagreement in the margin. This 'Combative Reading' forces the brain to stay engaged and prevents glazing over.
Identify one small concept from the reading and write it on a sticky note to be applied today (e.g., 'Practice better listening in my 11 AM seminar').
Mark a 'Star' in his training log for every day he completed the 15-minute block without touching his phone. This treats mental focus as a 'stat' similar to his gym PRs.

goal: Prepare and drink a 24oz 'Electrolyte-First' hydration mix every morning
description: Sunita is a 19-year-old student who lives in a high-rise dorm with poor ventilation. she wakes up every morning with a 'dehydration headache' and extreme lethargy. She has a habit of reaching for an extra-large, sugary iced coffee to 'cure' the headache, which leads to a jittery heart rate and a mid-morning crash. Her friction point is the 'Blandness Barrier'—she finds plain water nauseating in the morning and craves salt/sugar.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dorm tap water tastes metallic; limited fridge space for bottled water; tight student budget.
baseline.physical_constraints: Prone to morning migraines; low blood pressure.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must contain at least 500mg of sodium (from sea salt); zero added cane sugar; must be finished before any caffeine intake.

tasks.description: Fill a filtered water pitcher before bed. Cold water is easier to drink for her than lukewarm tap water. Place a clean 24oz jar next to the pitcher.
In the morning, add 1/4 tsp of sea salt and a squeeze of lemon to the 24oz of water. The salt treats the 'dehydration headache' more effectively than coffee would.
Carry the jar while walking to the bus. She must finish the entire 24oz before the bus arrives. Using the 'Deadline' of the bus schedule prevents her from sipping and forgetting.
Rate her head pain on a scale of 1-10 at 10:00 AM. Recognizing that her headache is a 1 instead of a 6 will solidify the salt-water habit over the cafe coffee.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily on 'Morning Sunlight and Movement' for circadian health
description: Noah is a 21-year-old student living in a basement-level apartment. He has 'Delayed Sleep Phase Syndrome,' meaning he naturally wants to sleep until noon. He misses his 9 AM classes and feels 'depressed' by the lack of light. His friction point is 'The Cave Reflex'—it is so dark and cold in his room that he feels a biological urge to stay under the covers for hours, scrolling his phone for warmth.

baseline.fixed_constraints: No direct sunlight in the apartment; must walk 10 minutes to reach the campus quad; heavy winter weather.
baseline.physical_constraints: Extreme morning grogginess (sleep inertia); seasonal affective disorder (SAD).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be physically outside (roof or quad) within 30 minutes of waking; no sunglasses allowed during the 10-minute light exposure.

tasks.description: Immediately upon the alarm, turn on every light in the room and a 10,000 lux SAD lamp. This 'fakes' the sunrise to trigger the cortisol awakening response.
Place a warm coat and boots directly next to the bed. The goal is to minimize the 'friction' of getting dressed so he can exit the 'cave' faster.
Walk to the campus quad and spend 10 minutes looking at the sky (not directly at the sun). This signals the retinas to reset his internal clock for the day.
At 11:00 PM, note how easy it was to fall asleep. He will notice that the morning walk makes him tired at a 'normal' time for the first time in years.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Reflective Problem Solving' for STEM assignments
description: Li-Wei is a 20-year-old engineering student who is 'fast but messy.' He rushes through his morning problem sets, makes simple arithmetic errors, and then gets frustrated when the final answer is wrong. His friction point is 'Speed Addiction'—he equates finishing quickly with intelligence, leading to a high 're-work' rate and a lack of deep conceptual understanding.

baseline.fixed_constraints: 60-minute bus commute; heavy use of a graphing calculator; limited scratch paper budget.
baseline.physical_constraints: Occasional hand cramping; eye strain from small-print textbooks.
baseline.non_negotiables: No calculator allowed for the first 10 minutes of problem-solving; must write down the 'logical steps' in plain English.

tasks.description: Open the problem set and read the first question three times slowly. Underline the 'Givens' and the 'Target.' This prevents the 'rushing' reflex from taking over.
Before calculating, write down a 'ballpark' answer (e.g., 'The result should be around 50'). This builds his intuitive 'Sense of Number' and catches errors later.
Write out the first step of the problem in a full sentence (e.g., 'I am going to find the derivative of X to determine the slope'). This forces logical clarity.
If he makes a mistake, he must categorize it: 'Arithmetic' or 'Concept.' If it's 'Arithmetic' 3 times in a row, he must perform 2 minutes of hand-stretches as a 'penalty reset'.

goal: Establish a 10-minute 'Identity-First' grooming routine
description: Jordan is a freshman who has fallen into a 'pajamas in public' habit. He rolls out of bed 5 minutes before class and attends lectures looking unkempt. He has noticed a decline in his self-respect and feels 'invisible' to his professors and peers. His friction point is 'The Comfort Trap'—he views grooming as a 'fake' or 'high-maintenance' activity that doesn't fit his 'chill student' persona.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shared communal bathroom (limited privacy); tight laundry budget; minimal wardrobe.
baseline.physical_constraints: Dorm-water related skin irritation (needs moisturizing).
baseline.non_negotiables: No wearing pajamas or sweatpants to any lecture; must brush teeth and wash face before 8:30 AM.

tasks.description: Before bed, hang one pair of clean jeans and a collared shirt or high-quality sweater on the back of the chair. This removes the 'I don't know what to wear' hurdle.
Splash cold water on the face for 30 seconds followed by a basic moisturizer. This treats the skin irritation and provides a sharp 'sensory signal' to the brain that the day has started.
After dressing, stand in front of the mirror for 30 seconds. Fix hair and check for a straight collar. Mentally say, 'I am a professional in training.'
Wipe down his sneakers or boots with a damp cloth. Clean shoes are a high-leverage way to feel 'put together' without buying a whole new wardrobe.

goal: Execute a 10-minute 'Anti-Sedentary' morning mobility routine
description: Jared is a 20-year-old architecture student who spends 10+ hours a day hunched over a drafting table or a laptop. He wakes up with a 'stiff' lower back and recurring tension headaches at the base of his skull. He feels 'too young' to have chronic pain and finds typical yoga classes 'too slow.' His friction point is 'Immediate Gratitude'—if he doesn't feel a 'crack' or immediate relief, he assumes the stretching isn't working and skips it.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dorm floor is carpeted but dusty; 9:00 AM studio start time; shares a room with a light sleeper.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tight hip flexors from prolonged sitting; forward head posture (tech-neck).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be silent (no jumping); no equipment required; must happen before he puts on his heavy backpack.

tasks.description: Use the edge of the dorm bed to perform a 2-minute lunge stretch per side. This targets the hip flexors that have 'shortened' from 10 hours of sitting.
Stand against the dorm door and slide arms up and down 15 times while keeping the lower back flat. This realigns the thoracic spine and counteracts the drafting-table hunch.
Perform 10 slow chin tucks (creating a 'double chin') while sitting at his desk. This strengthens the deep neck flexors to prevent tension headaches.
Rate morning back stiffness from 1-10. Noticing that the 'spasms' have decreased over 3 weeks will provide the logic-based motivation he needs.

goal: Complete 15 minutes of 'Active Recall' study session before checking any digital devices
description: Sasha is a 19-year-old freshman who 'bed-scrolls' on TikTok for 45 minutes every morning, which leaves her feeling overstimulated and behind. She struggles with 'Memory Decay' in her foreign language class. Her friction point is 'Dopamine Dependency'—the physical itch to check her phone is so strong that she feels 'boring' thoughts like study review are impossible to focus on without a digital reward.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 30 minutes by bus; high-stakes vocab quiz every Friday; shared bathroom schedule.
baseline.physical_constraints: Morning 'brain fog' that lasts until the first coffee.
baseline.non_negotiables: Phone must stay in a zipped bag in the closet; must use physical flashcards or a blank notepad; session must finish by 7:45 AM.

tasks.description: Place the stack of 10 'hard' vocab cards on her pillow before sleep. This ensures the physical cards are the first thing she touches in the morning, not the phone.
Attempt to recall the definition and kanji/script for each card. If she fails, she must 'struggle' to remember for 5 seconds before flipping.
Circle the items she missed in red. This visual marker identifies 'problem areas' for her bus commute later.
Only after the 15 minutes is up can she retrieve the phone. This trains the brain that 'effort comes before dopamine.'

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily on 'Morning Financial Inventory' to stop overdraft fees
description: Toby is a freshman who lives on a strict $50/week allowance. He has a 'fear of the number' and avoids his banking app, leading to unexpected $35 overdraft fees from $4 lattes. His friction point is 'Financial Shame'—he views a low balance as a personal failure rather than a data point, so he 'ostriches' (hides his head in the sand) until his card is declined.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited income; works 10 hours/week in the student union; high social pressure to spend on weekends.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but experiences 'gut-punch' anxiety when notifications from the bank appear.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must open the banking app every morning; no use of 'Buy Now Pay Later' apps; must track every dollar spent the day before.

tasks.description: Open the banking app for 30 seconds. Look at the balance and say the number out loud. This desensitizes the 'shame' response through repeated exposure to the data.
Divide his remaining weekly budget by the days left. (e.g., $20 left / 4 days = $5/day). Write this 'Spending Cap' on his hand or a sticky note.
Identify one transaction from yesterday that was 'invisible' (e.g., an automatic subscription or a service fee). Cancel one unused subscription per week.
At the end of the week, if his balance is above $0, he treats himself to one $1 item from the vending machine as a symbolic victory over the 'shame' cycle.

goal: Engage in 15 minutes of 'Social Priming' at the campus dining hall
description: Jin is a transfer student who feels like an outsider. He grabs a bagel and immediately retreats to his room to eat in isolation. He wants to make friends but feels that everyone is already in a 'clique.' His friction point is 'The Spotlight Effect'—he is convinced that if he sits alone at a large table, people are judging him for having no friends.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dining hall is peak-busy from 8:15 AM to 8:45 AM; limited seating; noisy environment.
baseline.physical_constraints: Social-anxiety induced nausea; tends to hunch shoulders and look down.
baseline.non_negotiables: No earbuds allowed in the dining hall; must sit at a communal table (at least 4 seats); phone must stay in the bag.

tasks.description: After getting food, stand by the water station for 60 seconds. Scan for a table with at least one empty seat. This allows the heart rate to stabilize before the social interaction.
Approach a table and ask: 'Is anyone sitting here?' This is a 3-second 'low-stakes' interaction that proves he is allowed to occupy the space.
Eat the meal with back straight and hands visible. No 'hiding' in his phone. Observe the room to see that 90% of people are focused on their own food, not him.
Give a brief nod to the person sitting across from him before leaving. No words needed. This builds 'social presence' without the pressure of a conversation.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily on 'Morning Administrative Clearance' for internships
description: Jordan is a 21-year-old senior who is great at his classes but 'melts down' when looking at internship applications. He has 80+ unread professional emails. His friction point is 'Task Avoidance'—he views a simple 'Schedule an Interview' email as a 2-hour ordeal involving checking his calendar, finding a suit, and researching the company.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must use the campus Career Center's portal; limited professional wardrobe; morning 'brain-fog'.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must respond to all 'Direct Messages' within 24 hours; no checking personal social media until 'Admin' is clear.

tasks.description: Search the inbox for keywords: 'Interview', 'Application', 'Offer'. Move these to a 'Priority' folder. Ignore everything else for this 15-minute block.
Reply to one scheduling request using a pre-written template: 'Hello [Name], I am available at [Time]. Thank you for the opportunity.' Do not overthink the prose.
Immediately mark the interview time on his physical wall calendar. This prevents the 'I forgot' panic that leads to ghosting.
Archive the handled email. Seeing the number of 'Priority' emails go down provides the visual proof that the task wasn't a two-hour ordeal.

goal: Spend 10 minutes every morning applying 'Interleaving' to solve three mixed-topic practice problems
description: Linh is a 20-year-old Mathematics major who excels at 'blocked' practice (doing 50 of the same type of problem) but fails exams because she can't identify which formula to use when topics are mixed. Her friction point is 'Cognitive Switching'—moving between different types of logic feels slow and exhausting, making her feel like she's 'losing her touch.' She often reverts to easy problems in the morning to feel a sense of accomplishment, which creates a false sense of security.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 40 minutes via train; 9:00 AM Linear Algebra lecture; limited access to a tutor.
baseline.physical_constraints: Severe motion sickness (cannot read on the train); requires a stable desk for writing.
baseline.non_negotiables: No solving problems from the same chapter twice in one session; must use a physical 'scratch' pad; session must be done before leaving the apartment.

tasks.description: Pick three problems from three different chapters of the textbook. Write them on a single sheet of paper the night before to eliminate 'decision fatigue' in the morning.
Before writing any numbers, spend 1 minute per problem just identifying *which* theorem or formula is required. This trains the 'recognition' muscle she misses during blocked practice.
Solve Step 1 of Problem A, then immediately switch to Step 1 of Problem B. This forced switching prevents the brain from entering 'autopilot' and mirrors the high-pressure environment of an exam.
If a switch feels 'jarring,' mark a check on the page. This checkmark represents 'effective learning' rather than a mistake, reframing the friction as progress.

goal: Engage in 15 minutes of 'Morning Digital Satiety' using only long-form educational content
description: Sam is a 19-year-old freshman who wakes up with 'Notification Anxiety.' He spends his first 30 minutes in bed scrolling short-form videos, which results in a fragmented attention span and high irritability during his 10 AM history seminar. His friction point is 'Boredom Intolerance'—without the rapid-fire dopamine of scrolling, he feels a sense of emptiness and dread, which he tries to fill with more scrolling.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dorm Wi-Fi is strongest in the morning; shared room (must use headphones); high academic pressure.
baseline.physical_constraints: Eyestrain from high-brightness screens in the dark; shallow 'anxiety' breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: No TikTok, Reels, or Shorts; phone must be in 'Do Not Disturb' mode; video must be at least 15 minutes long (long-form only).

tasks.description: The night before, select one 15-minute educational video (e.g., a documentary clip or a lecture) and have the tab ready. This prevents the 'search loop' that leads back to social media.
Sit up at the desk to watch the video; no watching in bed. The change in posture signals to the brain that this is 'active learning,' not 'passive numbing.'
Write down exactly one interesting thing learned from the video on a sticky note. This 'output' validates the time spent as productive.
Notice the urge to scroll. Instead of acting on it, take three deep breaths. This 'urge surfing' builds the muscle of self-control for the rest of the day.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Scenario-Based' verbal professional pitches
description: Javier is a 21-year-old Senior who mumbles during career fairs. He has a great GPA in Engineering but lacks 'Professional Fluency'—he struggles to explain his technical projects in a way that sounds confident. His friction point is 'Self-Consciousness'—he feels 'fake' or 'arrogant' when he speaks about his achievements, leading him to downplay his role in team successes.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Interviews coming up in 4 weeks; lives in a triple-dorm (no privacy for talking loud); high caffeine intake.
baseline.physical_constraints: Stutters slightly when nervous; avoids eye contact with his own reflection.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must record the audio of the pitch; must include one 'Value Statement' (e.g., 'I saved the team 5 hours by...'); no reading from a script.

tasks.description: While walking to the shower, whisper his 30-second 'Elevator Pitch.' This builds the muscle memory of the words without the pressure of an audience.
Record a 60-second explanation of one project. He must listen to it once. Hearing his own voice helps him identify 'filler words' (um, like, uh) that he currently misses.
Look at himself in the bathroom mirror and deliver the final sentence of his pitch. He must maintain eye contact for 3 seconds *after* he finishes speaking. This builds social confidence.
Write down: 'Facts are not bragging.' This addresses his friction point by reminding him that stating his contributions is a professional requirement, not an ego trip.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily on 'Morning Hydration and Gut-Health' maintenance
description: Fatima is a 20-year-old student who 'lives' on campus coffee and energy drinks. She has chronic bloating and morning stomach cramps, which make her feel sluggish in her 9 AM labs. She finds 'health trends' overwhelming and expensive. Her friction point is 'The Morning Rush'—she values those extra 15 minutes of sleep so much that she skips all nutrition until 1 PM.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Small dorm-sized fridge; no stove; shares a sink with 40 people; high-cost campus market.
baseline.physical_constraints: Lactose sensitivity; prone to caffeine-induced tremors.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be under $1.00 per serving; must be consumed before any caffeine; must involve at least 16oz of room-temperature water.

tasks.description: Place a 16oz bottle of water on the desk the night before. This eliminates the 'choice' and the walk to the communal sink in the morning.
Eat one low-cost, shelf-stable probiotic (e.g., a specific yogurt or a high-fiber bar) while drinking the water. This targets her bloating issues directly.
She must wait 15 minutes after finishing the water before heading to the campus cafe. This allows the hydration to settle and prevents the caffeine from hitting an empty stomach.
At 11:00 AM, record her stomach comfort (1-10). Seeing the correlation between morning water and reduced pain will make the 15-minute 'lost' sleep feel worth it.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Pre-Lecture' focused breathing and neck mobility
description: Zack is a 19-year-old student who spends 8 hours a day in non-ergonomic chairs. He wakes up with a 'stiff' neck and 'hunched' shoulders. He often 'snaps' at classmates because he’s in low-level pain all morning. His friction point is 'Gym Intimidation'—he thinks the only way to get fit is the campus gym, which he finds too loud and public, so he stays in pain.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor walk-up; limited floor space; uses a heavy 20lb backpack; high noise dorm.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (forward head posture); tight scalene muscles; shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be standing (no floor space); must be done before putting on the backpack; no equipment needed.

tasks.description: Stand against the back of the door and perform 15 chin tucks (creating a 'double chin'). This realigns the cervical spine after a night of poor pillow support.
Form a 'W' with the arms and squeeze the shoulder blades together for 10 seconds. Repeat 5 times. This opens the chest that has been 'collapsed' by drafting or typing.
Perform 4 rounds of Box Breathing (Inhale 4, Hold 4, Exhale 4, Hold 4) while standing. This physically lowers his stress baseline before he enters the crowded quad.
Tighten both backpack straps so the bag sits high on the back. This uses the weight of his books to pull his shoulders back rather than down.

goal: Spend 10 minutes every morning applying 'Spaced Repetition' to flashcards for midterms
description: Ben is a 19-year-old pre-law student who is drowning in Latin terminology and case law. He has a habit of 'cramming'—studying for 6 hours straight the night before a quiz—which leads to high stress and zero long-term retention. His friction point is 'Mental Friction'; he finds the initial struggle of remembering a card frustrating and often 'cheats' by looking at the answer too early. He also feels that 10 minutes is 'too short' to be productive, leading him to skip the habit entirely.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 30 minutes by bus; limited desk space in a quad-dorm; constant noise from roommates.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but prone to digital eye strain from excessive laptop use.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a dedicated Spaced Repetition System (SRS) app; cards must be reviewed before 10 AM to ensure the brain is fresh.
